original_abstract:
Traditional extraction methodologies frequently rely on flammable or hazardous organic solvents, driving a demand for sustainable alternatives capable of recovering bioactive compounds for food and nutraceutical applications. Natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) have emerged as promising non-toxic candidates to replace conventional hydroalcoholic extraction. These mixtures function through intricate intermolecular hydrogen bonding between their constituents, resulting in a eutectic point significantly lower than the melting points of the individual precursors. Typical NADES systems utilize aqueous solutions of choline chloride, various sugars, or polyols. This study explores the efficacy of two specific NADES—betaine:triethylene glycol (Bet:TEG) and choline chloride:1,2-propanediol (Chol:Prop)—as green solvents for extracting polyphenols from spent coffee grounds (SCG), a prevalent industrial byproduct.

Extraction yield and selectivity were rigorously assessed and compared against standard eco-friendly methods, including hot water and hydroalcoholic solutions. Furthermore, the study investigated how these eutectic media influenced the antioxidant and antimicrobial profiles of the resulting extracts. Key findings demonstrated that NADES achieved extraction efficiencies comparable to traditional green solvents while operating under milder temperatures and eliminating the need for flammable reagents. Notably, the antimicrobial potency of the NADES-based extracts was tenfold higher than that of aqueous or ethanolic counterparts. Due to their inherently low toxicity, these natural deep eutectic solvents offer significant potential as both extraction media and functional formulation aids in the development of novel food ingredients.
humanized_abstract:
Existing processes for extracting bioactive compounds for food and nutraceutical purposes involve the use of flammable and/or hazardous hydrophobic organic solvents. Thus, searching for safer alternatives is an urgent task. Recently, natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) have been proposed as non-toxic sustainable alternatives to replace conventional hydroalcoholic extraction. Generally, these mixtures are composed of molecules with multiple interactions like strong hydrogen bonds, which results in the decrease of the melting point to a eutectic point lower than the initial melting points of each pure compound. In most cases, the preparation of the mixtures only involves mixing aqueous solutions of choline chloride with sugars or polyols. Here, two brand new mixtures, namely betaine:triethylene glycol (Bet:TEG) and choline chloride:1,2-propanediol (Chol:Prop), are used for the first time as green solvents to extract polyphenols from spent coffee grounds (SCG).

Extraction yield and selectivity were studied and compared to well-known “green” methods such as hot water and hydroalcoholic solutions. Their influence on antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the extracts was also evaluated. The results showed that NADES can afford extraction yields and selectivities similar to those obtained with “green” extraction methods but at milder temperatures and by avoiding the use of flammable solvents. The antimicrobial activity of extracts obtained with NADES is tenfold higher than those obtained with aqueous or ethanolic solutions. Thus, due to their low toxicity, NADES can be used not only as extraction media but also as functional ingredients or actives formulation aids for the development of food additives.
